EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds surged behind a strong arctic cold front which moved across the region early on January 16. The strongest winds were generally west of the James River Valley where gusts to 60 to 70 mph were common. While areas of light snowfall generally on the order from a dusting to an inch fell before and concurrent with the strong winds, there were only very spotty and brief instances of visibility below one mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 45 mph at times with a peak gust of 60 mph at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Tripp and 59 mph 4 miles south of Freeman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second round of high winds and snow showers created hazardous travel conditions across northeast South Dakota. Although snow accumulations were minor, totaling only 2 inches or less, the light snow led to slick roads and intermittent near-blizzard conditions. These conditions resulted in several accidents, including a vehicle rollover on Interstate 90 near Vivian and a jackknifed semi-trailer on Highway 12 west of Aberdeen. The highest wind gust of 63 mph was recorded by South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet stations near Roscoe and Bowdle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A two vehicle accident limited travel for a portion of Highway 212 5 miles east of Clark. The South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station 7 miles east of Clark reported a peak wind gust of 31 mph at 2pm with snow showers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second round of high winds and snow showers created hazardous travel conditions across northeast South Dakota. Although snow accumulations were minor, totaling only 2 inches or less, the light snow led to slick roads and intermittent near-blizzard conditions. These conditions resulted in several accidents, including a vehicle rollover on Interstate 90 near Vivian and a jackknifed semi-trailer on Highway 12 west of Aberdeen. The highest wind gust of 63 mph was recorded by South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet stations near Roscoe and Bowdle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Day County Sheriff reported blizzard conditions and advised against travel. The South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station in Andover recorded a peak gust of 43 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak weather system combined with an arctic blast to bring high winds and light snow to the region on the morning of January 21. The Pierre Regional Airport ASOS measured the strongest wind gust at 76 mph. Matching 75 mph gusts were recorded by a personal weather station north of Pierre and the South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station southeast of Reliance. While the Watertown COOP recorded 2.4 inches of snow, most other locations received an inch or less. The combination of wind and snow prompted No Travel Advised notices for Walworth, Hand, Faulk, Spink, Roberts, and Hamlin counties. Slick roads and intermittent low visibility resulted in a number of slide-ins across Roberts county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 30 to 38 mph at times with a peak gust of 53 mph at a South Dakota Mesonet site 7 miles west-southwest of Beresford and an Automated Weather Observing System site at Vermillion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds behind yet another arctic front reached speeds from 35 to 55 mph with gusts as high as 75 mph across southeast South Dakota. When periods of light snowfall occurred that would total between one and two inches north of Highway 18, gusty winds produced considerable near-blizzard and very brief blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds were sustained from 40 to 55 mph with gusts as high as 73 mph 13 miles west of Platte, 67 mph 5 miles east of Wagner, 66 mph 2 miles west of Geddes, and 62 mph at Wagner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system and arctic blast brought strong winds and pockets of light to moderate snow showers.  ||A peak wind gust of 71 mph was observed 1 mile north northwest of Lees Corner in Buffalo County by the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) Road Information Weather System (RWIS), and by a personal weather station 2 miles north of the Pierre Regional Airport. A 68 mph wind gust was also recorded by the SDDOT RWIS station 1 mile northeast of Stephan in Hyde county, while 66 mph wind gusts were measured by the SDDOT RWIS stations 5 miles south southwest of Klein Ranch in Corson county and 1 mile northeast of Timber Lake in Dewey county.  A total of 39 weather stations recorded a peak wind gust at or in excess of 58 mph. These high winds flipped a semi and damaged a grain elevator near the town of Murdo on the morning of the 16th. ||While snow accumulations were limited, snow showers of moderate intensity combined with these strong winds to produce temporary white-out conditions. No Travel Advised was issued by both the Walworth and Day county Sheriffs. Various SDDOT RWIS webcams showed visibility in blowing snow fluctuating significantly over short distances in the matter of a few minutes. An emergency vehicle rendering assistance for an accident on Interstate 29 near Watertown was struck with minor injuries. A separate accident along Interstate 90 resulted in a 5 vehicle chain reaction crash which resulted in 2 fatalities and a number of injuries.  ||80 residents of Deuel county reported power out during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system and arctic blast brought strong winds and pockets of light to moderate snow showers.  ||A peak wind gust of 71 mph was observed 1 mile north northwest of Lees Corner in Buffalo County by the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) Road Information Weather System (RWIS), and by a personal weather station 2 miles north of the Pierre Regional Airport. A 68 mph wind gust was also recorded by the SDDOT RWIS station 1 mile northeast of Stephan in Hyde county, while 66 mph wind gusts were measured by the SDDOT RWIS stations 5 miles south southwest of Klein Ranch in Corson county and 1 mile northeast of Timber Lake in Dewey county.  A total of 39 weather stations recorded a peak wind gust at or in excess of 58 mph. These high winds flipped a semi and damaged a grain elevator near the town of Murdo on the morning of the 16th. ||While snow accumulations were limited, snow showers of moderate intensity combined with these strong winds to produce temporary white-out conditions. No Travel Advised was issued by both the Walworth and Day county Sheriffs. Various SDDOT RWIS webcams showed visibility in blowing snow fluctuating significantly over short distances in the matter of a few minutes. An emergency vehicle rendering assistance for an accident on Interstate 29 near Watertown was struck with minor injuries. A separate accident along Interstate 90 resulted in a 5 vehicle chain reaction crash which resulted in 2 fatalities and a number of injuries.  ||80 residents of Deuel county reported power out during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near the South Shore exit on Interstate 90, while rendering assistance for a slide-in, the emergency vehicle was struck by another vehicle, resulting in significant damage to both and minor injuries for the occupants.||The accident was caused by strong winds, low visibility, and icy roads. Between 2:00 PM and 2:30 PM, the nearby Watertown ASOS reported visibility below one mile in snow showers, with wind gusts ranging from 35 to 43 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak weather system combined with an arctic blast to bring high winds and light snow to the region on the morning of January 21. The Pierre Regional Airport ASOS measured the strongest wind gust at 76 mph. Matching 75 mph gusts were recorded by a personal weather station north of Pierre and the South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station southeast of Reliance. While the Watertown COOP recorded 2.4 inches of snow, most other locations received an inch or less. The combination of wind and snow prompted No Travel Advised notices for Walworth, Hand, Faulk, Spink, Roberts, and Hamlin counties. Slick roads and intermittent low visibility resulted in a number of slide-ins across Roberts county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Day county Sheriff issued No Travel Advised for Day county with the visibility near zero at times thanks to the combination of snow showers and strong winds. The South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet stations across the county recorded wind gusts generally between 35 and 45 mph, with a peak wind gust of 54 mph recorded at the Andover station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure moving into the area resulted in extreme cold and dangerous wind chills. By Friday morning, much of the area was seeing temperatures of at least 20 degrees below zero, and as cold as 25 degrees below zero measured at the South Dakota State University mesonet stations in Gettysburg and 23 miles north of Highmore. With these cold temperatures and a northwest breeze, wind chills across much of the area had fallen to 40 degrees below zero and colder, with the South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station in Summit coming in with a wind chill of 53 degrees below zero. Temperatures failed to warm much during the day, with highs in the single digits to teens below zero. Watertown set a record for the lowest daily high temperature, reaching only 11 degrees below zero.

Northwest winds surged behind a strong arctic cold front which moved across the region early on January 16. The strongest winds were generally west of the James River Valley where gusts to 60 to 70 mph were common. While areas of light snowfall generally on the order from a dusting to an inch fell before and concurrent with the strong winds, there were only very spotty and brief instances of visibility below one mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 43 mph at times with a peak gust of 61 mph at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site at Kimball.

A clipper system and arctic blast brought strong winds and pockets of light to moderate snow showers.  ||A peak wind gust of 71 mph was observed 1 mile north northwest of Lees Corner in Buffalo County by the South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) Road Information Weather System (RWIS), and by a personal weather station 2 miles north of the Pierre Regional Airport. A 68 mph wind gust was also recorded by the SDDOT RWIS station 1 mile northeast of Stephan in Hyde county, while 66 mph wind gusts were measured by the SDDOT RWIS stations 5 miles south southwest of Klein Ranch in Corson county and 1 mile northeast of Timber Lake in Dewey county.  A total of 39 weather stations recorded a peak wind gust at or in excess of 58 mph. These high winds flipped a semi and damaged a grain elevator near the town of Murdo on the morning of the 16th. ||While snow accumulations were limited, snow showers of moderate intensity combined with these strong winds to produce temporary white-out conditions. No Travel Advised was issued by both the Walworth and Day county Sheriffs. Various SDDOT RWIS webcams showed visibility in blowing snow fluctuating significantly over short distances in the matter of a few minutes. An emergency vehicle rendering assistance for an accident on Interstate 29 near Watertown was struck with minor injuries. A separate accident along Interstate 90 resulted in a 5 vehicle chain reaction crash which resulted in 2 fatalities and a number of injuries.  ||80 residents of Deuel county reported power out during the event.

Arctic high pressure moving into the area resulted in extreme cold and dangerous wind chills. By Friday morning, much of the area was seeing temperatures of at least 20 degrees below zero, and as cold as 25 degrees below zero measured at the South Dakota State University mesonet stations in Gettysburg and 23 miles north of Highmore. With these cold temperatures and a northwest breeze, wind chills across much of the area had fallen to 40 degrees below zero and colder, with the South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station in Summit coming in with a wind chill of 53 degrees below zero. Temperatures failed to warm much during the day, with highs in the single digits to teens below zero. Watertown set a record for the lowest daily high temperature, reaching only 11 degrees below zero.

Northwest winds surged behind a strong arctic cold front which moved across the region early on January 16. The strongest winds were generally west of the James River Valley where gusts to 60 to 70 mph were common. While areas of light snowfall generally on the order from a dusting to an inch fell before and concurrent with the strong winds, there were only very spotty and brief instances of visibility below one mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 46 mph at times with a peak gust of 59 mph at a Automated Surface Observing System site at Huron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second round of high winds and snow showers created hazardous travel conditions across northeast South Dakota. Although snow accumulations were minor, totaling only 2 inches or less, the light snow led to slick roads and intermittent near-blizzard conditions. These conditions resulted in several accidents, including a vehicle rollover on Interstate 90 near Vivian and a jackknifed semi-trailer on Highway 12 west of Aberdeen. The highest wind gust of 63 mph was recorded by South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet stations near Roscoe and Bowdle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a vehicle rollover on Interstate 90, west of Vivian. The incident was caused by high winds and light snow which led to poor visibility in the area, along with icy road conditions. A peak wind gust of 54 mph was recorded on the South Dakota State University mesonet station 12 miles south of Vivian at 12:50 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Arctic high pressure departed, cold temperatures and a south breeze resulted in locally dangerous wind chills. Summit recorded a wind chill of 45 degrees below zero, with 41 degrees below zero recorded at the South Dakota Department of Transportation weather stations near Peever, Altamont and Brandt.

A locally strong wind event occurred in far northeast South Dakota during the morning of January 26th. The South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station 6 miles south southwest of Peever recorded a peak wind gust of 67 mph.
